system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: Section One/Part 2: Learning the Blues: Fretted Notes, Rests

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 2 — Section One/Part 2: Learning the Blues: Fretted Notes, Rests

_Pages 36-49._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Dotted Note Extension Rule

> A dot after a note extends the note for precisely half as long as the note itself. In 4/4
> time a half note normally receives two beats. If it were dotted, it would receive three beats, as in the
> example below.

**Significance (p. 36).** Defines foundational rule for dotted notes that governs all duration calculations.

## Dot vs Tie Distinction

> Although dotted notes and ties are both used as note extenders, there is a difference. A dot
> after a note always extends the note by half the original value of the note. A note with a tie attached
> to the proceeding note on the right extends the original note by the value of the second note. When
> music is notated, the choice between using dotted notes over tied notes is determined by the ease
> of readability.

**Significance (p. 36).** Distinguishes two easily-confused notation tools and gives rule for choosing between them.

## Two-Part Rhythm Technique

> RH: i-m or m-a, rest stroke; p, free stroke

**Significance (p. 37).** Specifies the right-hand technique combining melodic and bass voices, a core fingerstyle coordination pattern.

## Eighth Note Notation

> Individual eighth notes have a head, stem, and a single flag. Note how the eighth note rest differs
> from the quarter note rest.

**Significance (p. 39).** Introduces the visual anatomy of eighth notes essential to reading jazz melodies.

## Jazz Pulse and Melody Relationship

> The basic underlying pulse of jazz is a quarter note, on top of which is a melody based on a steady
> pulse of eighth notes.

**Significance (p. 39).** Defines the structural two-layer rhythmic architecture of jazz central to the entire method.

## Counting Eighth Notes

> In 4/4 time, or any meter in which a
> quarter note receives one beat (X/4), an
> eighth note or eighth rest receives a half
> beat. By convention, an eighth note falling
>
> ona beat is counted with 1, 2, 3 or 4, and an
>
> eighth note falling in between or offthe beat
> is counted with the word and.
>
> Eighth notes

**Significance (p. 40).** Establishes the 1-and-2-and counting system underpinning all subsequent exercises.

## Self-Monitoring While Counting

> Try counting the eighth-note subdivisions aloud, especially in trouble spots. If you listen to
> yourself as you count, you will most likely spot any inaccuracies. For instance, if you listen and
> find that at a certain point you are changing the speed of your counting to match what you think
> the music is asking you to play, stop and check. The pace or tempo of the underlying basic beat
> should always be the same, of course. Pencil in the counting if necessary.

**Significance (p. 40).** Diagnostic technique for self-correcting rhythmic errors.

## Swing/Shuffle Feel Definition

> But an authentic idiomatic interpretation would require eighth notes on 1, 2, 3 and 4 to be
> slightly longer in duration, and notes on and to be proportionally shorter. In early jazz, an accent
> was placed on 1 and 3. Later the place of emphasis changed, landing on 2 and 4. For now, stay
> with 1 and 3. This long-short feeling is called shuffle, or swing. It is also the feeling underlying the
> songs of rhythm and blues. The eighth-note feel will not show up in notes with a greater duration
> than eighth notes.

**Significance (p. 42).** Defines swing/shuffle, the defining rhythmic characteristic of jazz guitar.

## Swing Tempo Limitation

> This shuffle rhythm works best for slower-to-medium blues and jazz. When the tempo is very
> fast, play eighth notes more evenly.

**Significance (p. 42).** Prevents misapplying swing feel at fast tempos, linking rhythm interpretation to musical context.

## 4/4 Readability as 2/4+2/4

> Clear writing of music notation in a measure of 4/4 is organized rhythmically with 2/4 + 2/4 in mind.
> Look at the two lines of music below. They are rhythmically the same. Each measure of the top line
> is configured as though it were two half measures of two beats each. The second line is not incor-
> rect, but it is slightly more difficult to read. Keep this in mind when you begin writing your own music
> in Section Two of this

**Significance (p. 46).** Compositional/notation rule about thinking in half-measures for readability.

## Dotted Quarter Note Value

> In 4/4 time, a dotted half note receives three beats. Likewise, a
>
> dotted quarter note extends by a half beat, receiving a total of a beat and a half.

**Significance (p. 46).** Extends dotted-note rule to common quarter-note level.

## Tie vs Dot Across Measures

> Both ties and dotted notes extend the duration of notes, and in most cases are interchangeable,
> except across measures, where ties must be used.

**Significance (p. 46).** States the hard rule distinguishing when a tie is mandatory rather than optional.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
